challenge_prompt: A 30-year-old male patient presented with a 6-year history of drug-resistant epilepsy. He initially had occasional nocturnal facial muscle twitching without loss of consciousness, which later progressed to generalized tonic-clonic seizures once monthly and daily brief absence episodes despite oxcarbazepine treatment. Cranial MRI revealed an ovoid abnormal signal lesion in the left temporal pole, approximately 8 mm in diameter, showing isointensity on T1WI, hypointensity on T2WI and T2 FLAIR, isointensity on DWI, and no abnormal enhancement on contrast-enhanced scan. The initial imaging diagnosis suggested possible cavernous hemangioma. 24-hour video polysomnography-EEG showed abnormal discharges originating from the left anterior temporal lobe, involving the frontal pole and sphenoidal electrode regions, consistent with focal epileptic seizures. Left temporal lobe lesionectomy was performed. Intraoperatively, the lesion appeared as a grayish-white, firm mass with extensive calcification; the boundary was relatively well defined; no perilesional edema, abnormal neovascularization, or superficial hemosiderin deposition was noted. Postoperative histopathology showed brain cortex and white matter with focal glial proliferation, extensive calcification, mild cytological atypia, oval-to-spindle-shaped tumor cells, and focal small-vessel proliferation. Immunohistochemistry: GFAP+, Olig-2+, IDH-1 R132H negative, wild-type p53, retained ATRX, H3K27M negative, retained H3K27me3 and MTAP expression, CD34 diffuse positive, weak BRAF V600E VE1 immunoreactivity, and Ki-67 proliferation index <1%. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next management step?
title: Polymorphous low-grade neuroepithelial tumor of the young: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 28-year-old male presents with a two-week history of auditory hallucinations and paranoid delusions. challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old Chinese woman presents with frequent severe headaches, binaural tinnitus, and hearing loss. Her headaches began at age 20, are dull and distending, triggered by emotional stress or fatigue, and have increased in frequency. She has progressive memory loss. Cranial nerves normal, limb strength 5/5, sensation intact, reflexes normal, bilateral Babinski and Chaddock signs present. challenge_prompt:
A 39-year-old woman with 14 years of schooling, previously independent and employed as a saleswoman, presents with a six-year history of progressive cognitive decline and gait impairment. Initially, she had impoverished speech and slowed gait, including difficulty climbing stairs. Over five years, she continued living alone but had trouble securing employment due to psychomotor slowness. One year before evaluation, after a possible dengue virus infection, she experienced rapid deterioration in cognition and motor skills. Her gait became markedly unstable, requiring wheelchair use for most of the day, with significant trunk and limb incoordination. She also developed auditory/visual hallucinations and persecutory delusions. 

Medical history: recurrent migraines since adolescence (3–4 episodes weekly) and irregular menstrual cycles predating neurological symptoms. No comorbidities or toxic substance exposure. Family history: consanguineous parents (first cousins); a sister with similar symptom onset at age 2, rapid progression, and death at age 19; an older brother is asymptomatic.

Neurological examination: right hemifacial paresis; global muscle strength grade IV (MRC scale); hyperreflexia in all limbs; bilateral extensor plantar reflex; magnetic gait; dystonic posture in upper limbs; dysmetria in both upper and lower extremities; sensory intact; funduscopy normal. Cognitive assessment showed remarkable impairment (details not provided). She is dependent in instrumental activities of daily living but independent in basic activities.

Laboratory investigations: elevated follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and low estradiol (<5 ng/dL), consistent with primary ovarian failure. Serological, rheumatological, and metabolic panels (calcium, copper, iron studies) unremarkable. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis normal.

Brain MRI: diffuse, symmetric cerebral white matter abnormalities (T2 hyperintensity, T1 hypointensity) and frontal cystic lesions on FLAIR sequences. No gadolinium enhancement or calcifications on SWI.

Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?
title: When exome analysis is the key for your patient with cognitive decline: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 19-day-old female neonate presented with sudden poor feeding, perioral cyanosis, and poor responsiveness. She had recurrent apnea without respiratory effort, with desaturation to 65% and bradycardia, each episode resolving with tactile stimulation but recurring frequently. She was born at 39 weeks via C-section, unremarkable prenatal and family history. Physical exam: flat anterior fontanelle, clear lungs, no murmurs, soft abdomen, warm extremities. Neurological exam: normal tone, symmetric movements, intact reflexes, no focal deficits. Mildly decreased activity between apnea episodes. Laboratory: WBC 7,400/µL, Hb 13.0 g/dL, platelets 285 × 10³/µL, normal liver enzymes, CRP 0.53 mg/dL (mildly elevated), Na 132 mEq/L, K 4.9, Cl 98, normal glucose and ammonia. Venous blood gas: pH 7.308, pCO2 62.1 mmHg, HCO3 31.1 mmol/L (respiratory acidosis). CSF: no pleocytosis, normal protein and glucose, FilmArray meningitis panel negative. Chest X-ray: normal on day 1, but on day 2 showed infiltrative shadows in right upper lung and hilar region. Nasopharyngeal swab FilmArray respiratory panel positive for human rhinovirus/enterovirus. Blood, sputum, CSF cultures negative. Echocardiogram: no congenital heart disease, normal function. Non-contrast brain CT: normal. Continuous EEG during midazolam sedation: low-voltage background, poorly developed sleep-wake cycling, no epileptiform discharges. Apnea persisted despite mechanical ventilation. On hospital day 5, brain MRI showed hyperintense lesions on DWI in the genu of the corpus callosum and bilateral frontal white matter, with corresponding low ADC values; no intracranial mass or brainstem abnormalities. No specific treatment for the MRI findings was given. Apnea resolved on day 6, and the patient was extubated on the same day.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

A follow-up brain MRI on day 14 showed complete resolution of the previous DWI hyperintensities in the genu of the corpus callosum and frontal white matter.
title: Reversible Lesions of the Genu of the Corpus Callosum and White Matter Detected by MRI in a Neonate With Apnea: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 59-year-old Asian male with hyperlipidemia, fatty liver disease, and hyperuricemia (untreated) presents with progressive left facial and gingival swelling and numbness over 20 days. Initially, he awakened with left facial and gingival swelling and a sensation of bruxism. Within one week, he developed numbness involving the left half of the face (perioral region to vertex) and nocturnal drooling. He visited a dentist; non-contrast CT of oral floor, maxillofacial region, and salivary glands showed mild widening of left submandibular and sublingual ducts, no abscess, sialolith, or mass. The dentist diagnosed trigeminal or facial neuritis and prescribed mecobalamin and oral prednisone, with no improvement (numbness slightly worsened). On examination: alert, oriented, BP 115/85. Cranial nerves: selective sensory loss in left trigeminal distribution—markedly decreased pinprick and temperature over left cheek, upper lip, lower lip, and gingiva; partially preserved touch. Masticatory muscle strength normal, no jaw deviation, jaw reflex normal. No facial weakness. Other cranial nerves intact. Motor: normal tone and strength, no ataxia, no pyramidal signs. No hemisensory loss outside trigeminal area. Reflexes symmetric, flexor plantars. Gait steady. Labs: CBC, coagulation, LFTs, renal function, ANA, ANCA, HIV, syphilis, EBV, CMV all normal. Brain MRI (T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI, ADC): FLAIR shows focal hyperintensity in left pontine tegmentum; DWI shows focal diffusion-restricted hyperintensity along left pontine tegmentum, anatomical course of trigeminal sensory nucleus and fibers; ADC shows no corresponding hypointensity. No periventricular or subcortical white matter hyperintensities. No other diffusion-restricted lesions. MR angiography: no significant stenosis or occlusion of major intracranial vessels; mild atherosclerotic changes. The patient has no history of hypertension, diabetes, smoking, or alcohol. Family history unremarkable.
title: Pontine infarction isolated to trigeminal sensory fibers: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old woman with a known history of Crohn’s disease complicated by a perianal fistula, diagnosed by biopsy in 2021, presents with worsening neurological symptoms over two months. She was previously treated with azathioprine and corticosteroids, then transitioned to infliximab therapy every eight weeks. She has no personal or family history of neurological disease. Symptoms began with gradual bilateral proximal lower-limb weakness (difficulty rising from seated position, climbing stairs), progressing to impaired ambulation, along with dizziness and bifrontal tension-type headache. No nausea, vomiting, photophobia, fever, sensory loss, bowel/bladder dysfunction, visual loss, diplopia, seizures, loss of consciousness, recent infection, vaccination, trauma, or constitutional symptoms. Family noted progressive gait instability and slowness of speech. On exam, patient is conscious, alert, oriented. Neurological exam: wide-based ataxic gait, dysarthria, intention tremor; motor exam normal (normal tone and power, no proximal weakness); reflexes brisk especially on right; positive right Babinski sign; sensation normal; cranial nerves intact; no meningeal signs. Initial labs (CBC, kidney/liver panels, electrolytes, arterial blood gas) normal. Autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB, anti-proteinase-3, rheumatoid factor) negative. Endocrine evaluation: panhypopituitarism (low FSH, low LH, high prolactin, low vitamin D); thyroid tests confirm hypothyroidism (known history). Brain MRI: multiple hyperintense T2/FLAIR signal lesions in bilateral periventricular and deep white matter, involving thalami, internal capsules, corpus callosum, cerebellum, cerebellar peduncles, brainstem, dentate nuclei, predominantly symmetrical distribution. Whole spine MRI: no spinal cord lesions. No gadolinium enhancement status available; no black holes, optic nerve involvement, central vein sign, or paramagnetic rim lesions. EKG normal. Transthoracic echocardiogram normal. Lumbar puncture: normal opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose; no pleocytosis; oligoclonal bands absent. Serum aquaporin-4 antibodies negative; serum MOG antibodies negative. CT chest, abdomen, pelvis: no malignancy or lymphadenopathy. Based on this case, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next management step?
title: Infliximab-associated multifocal central nervous system demyelination mimicking multiple sclerosis in a patient with Crohn’s disease: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 52-year-old man with a 27-year history of schizophrenia, maintained on clozapine 150 mg/day and risperidone 2 mg/day, presented with sudden mutism and decreased responsiveness 12 days ago. On admission, he was found to have pulmonary embolism (multiple filling defects on CTA) and left lower leg intermuscular vein thrombosis. He also had a pulmonary infection. Initial treatment in the ICU included anti-infective therapy (cefoperazone/sulbactam) and low-molecular-weight heparin for DVT. Upon transfer to psychiatry, he was in a passive state with a nasogastric tube and urinary catheter. Mental status: dull gaze, uncooperative, poor eye contact, occasionally responded by nodding/shaking head. Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale (BFCRS) baseline score was 20. Risperidone was tapered and discontinued, and sertraline 50-150 mg/day was started due to reported low mood. He initially improved (more responsive, coherent) but soon relapsed into immobile, mute, negativistic state with fixed open stare, profuse sweating, sustained flexion of both upper extremities (BFCRS 18). Family denied prominent depressive symptoms at onset, so sertraline was tapered and sulpiride 100-400 mg/day started. He remained in catatonic stupor (BFCRS 21). Sulpiride led to partial improvement (brief conversation, passive walking) but dose escalation caused dysautonomia with profuse sweating, paroxysmal muscle tension and spasms lasting 1-2 hours. Ambulatory EEG showed no abnormalities. Sulpiride was reduced, but catatonia worsened: eyes closed, mute, immobile, no response to supraorbital pressure (BFCRS 24). Modified electroconvulsive therapy (MECT) was considered but deemed extremely high risk due to history of pulmonary embolism and lower limb DVT (risk of thrombus dislodgement and fatal PE). Based on this clinical scenario, what is the most appropriate next management step?
title: Diazepam combined with aripiprazole in the treatment of a catatonic stupor patient with venous thrombosis: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 23-year-old African-American woman with no significant prior medical history presented to the emergency department after experiencing seizure-like activity. She reported no preceding chest pain or palpitations. Prior evaluation for a seizure disorder included normal brain MRI and CT, and she was started on levetiracetam. Initial 12-lead ECG in the hospital showed normal sinus rhythm with QTc 393 ms. Continuous video EEG monitoring was initiated. During admission, she had another seizure-like episode; simultaneous telemetry and video EEG confirmed polymorphic ventricular tachycardia consistent with torsades de pointes (TdP), and EEG showed no epileptiform activity. A subsequent ECG showed a markedly prolonged QTc of 496 ms. Schwartz score was 5 (QTc ≥480 ms and TdP). Transthoracic echocardiogram and cardiac MRI were normal, showing normal biventricular size and function with no scar or inflammation. A comprehensive 51-gene arrhythmia and cardiomyopathy panel was negative for pathogenic variants. An implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) and implantable loop recorder (ILR) were placed. ILR interrogation documented a spontaneous arrhythmic event: sinus rhythm with a normal QT interval abruptly transitioning into a disorganized, polymorphic ventricular rhythm arising abruptly from a normal QT interval and distinct from the earlier torsades de pointes. The rhythm persisted until terminated by an ICD shock. Based on this presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?
title: Diagnostic Evolution From Channelopathy to Idiopathic Ventricular Fibrillation After Aborted Sudden Cardiac Death

challenge_prompt: A 63-year-old female with relapsed IgA lambda multiple myeloma (ISS stage 2, high-risk cytogenetics t(14;16)) underwent ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel) CAR-T therapy after prior treatments including CyBorD, autologous HCT, and daratumumab-based therapy. She received standard lymphodepletion with fludarabine/cyclophosphamide. On day +10, she developed fever (101°F) and grade 1 CRS, treated with tocilizumab and dexamethasone, but hypotension required norepinephrine. On day +11, acute left neck and eye pain occurred, self-resolved. Days +12-13: high absolute lymphocyte count (5.88K/µL, 4.72K/µL). On day +14, midodrine 2.5mg started for orthostatic hypotension. Days +14-15: recurrent undifferentiated shock. On day +19, she awoke with inability to move legs, weak extremities, loss of fine motor skills, and sudden severe headache behind eyes (peak <1 min, lasted hours). Symptoms resolved that night; CT head normal. Next day, exam showed unsteadiness and reduced attention. MRI brain day +20 suggested possible ischemic stroke vs ICANS (T2 hyperintensity, restricted diffusion). CT angiogram on day +20 showed multifocal severe stenosis throughout cerebral vasculature (A2, M2, P2 branches, cervical vertebral arteries) with beaded appearance, suggestive of vasculitis vs RCVS. Neurologic symptoms fluctuated, then on day +22 became persistent with Gerstmann's syndrome (right/left disorientation, finger agnosia, acalculia, agraphia) and Balint's syndrome (optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia, simultanagnosia). Diagnostic cerebral angiography day +22 showed diffuse beaded appearance of intracranial vasculature; no improvement after intra-arterial verapamil. Two serial lumbar punctures (48h apart) showed no nucleated cells, normal protein, ≤32 RBCs; negative infectious workup. Serum ANCA, ANA, cryoglobulins negative. CSF cytokine panel: IL-6 14.2 pg/mL (ref <7.5). Treatment for ICANS with high-dose IV methylprednisolone, anakinra, siltuximab started day +23. Serum cytokines day +23: IL-10 11.1 pg/mL (ref <11.1), IL-6 2.1 pg/mL (ref <2.0). On day +24, MRI showed worsening strokes with petechial hemorrhage. ALC 393 cells/µL, 72.3% BCMA-CAR-T positive. Cyclophosphamide and IVIG given. On day +26, CTA showed interval improvement in multifocal stenosis. MRI vessel wall imaging on day +28 showed no wall enhancement. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis/etiology, and what is the next appropriate management step?
title: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome following ciltacabtagene autoleucel therapy for relapsed multiple myeloma: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 65-year-old North African male with idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy was referred for CRT-D implantation. He presented with NYHA class III symptoms, LVEF of 28% by Simpson’s biplane method, and a QRS duration of 162 ms with left bundle branch block morphology in sinus rhythm. His medical history included hypertension managed with ramipril 5 mg daily, type 2 diabetes with HbA1c of 7.1% on metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and a 30-pack-year smoking history (ceased five years prior). Renal function was preserved (eGFR 72 mL/min/1.73 m2, serum creatinine 0.90 mg/dL). No drug allergies were reported. The procedure was performed under local anaesthesia with 2% lidocaine and light sedation using 2 mg intravenous midazolam. Venous access was via the left subclavian vein. A total of 50 mL of iopamidol (370 mgI/mL, low-osmolar, non-ionic) was administered in 5–10 mL boluses to visualize the coronary sinus ostium and branches. Leads were successfully positioned in the right atrial appendage, right ventricular apex, and a posterolateral branch of the coronary sinus using a quadripolar lead. Electrical parameters were optimal (capture thresholds <1.0 V at 0.4 ms, sensing >8 mV, impedances 600–800 Ω). Fluoroscopy duration was 14 min, with no intraoperative complications. Post-procedure chest X-ray confirmed correct lead positioning. Four hours after the procedure, the patient developed sudden agitation accompanied by rhythmic, involuntary jerking movements involving flexion and extension of the upper and lower limbs, exacerbated by tactile or auditory stimuli. Neurological examination revealed GCS 15/15, no cranial nerve deficits, preserved muscle strength, symmetrical hyperreflexia without Babinski sign, and no sensory level. Vital signs: BP 140/80 mmHg, HR 78 bpm (biventricular pacing), SpO2 96% on room air. Laboratory analysis showed mild troponin I elevation to 0.07 ng/mL (normal <0.04), normal electrolytes, and stable serum creatinine at 0.88 mg/dL. ECG demonstrated effective biventricular pacing with QRS narrowing to 118 ms. Electroencephalography at symptom onset revealed a normal posterior dominant alpha rhythm of 9–10 Hz without epileptiform discharges, slowing, or asymmetry. Due to initial unavailability of MRI, brain and cervical-thoracic spinal MRI were conducted on day 3 using a 1.5 Tesla scanner (T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI), demonstrating no acute ischaemic, haemorrhagic, or inflammatory lesions and no abnormal contrast enhancement within the spinal cord. Serum creatinine remained stable at 0.87 mg/dL on day 2 and 0.85 mg/dL on day 3. Lumbar puncture was not performed. Based on the clinical presentation and investigations, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
title: Spinal myoclonus post-CRT-D implantation: a rare case report of iodinated contrast neurotoxicity

challenge_prompt: A 64-year-old woman with a history of hypertension, asthma, and right-sided hemiplegia from a prior hemorrhagic stroke presented 1 week after onset of painful vesicular lesions over her right flank (T10 dermatome). She was diagnosed with herpes zoster (HZ) and completed a 7-day course of oral famciclovir. Due to persistent severe pain with open wounds, she was referred. At initial presentation, ESR was 54 mm/h and CRP 9.50 mg/L; WBC was normal. She was treated for secondary bacterial infection with ceftriaxone and oral cefditoren, with partial improvement. Two weeks later (approximately 4 weeks after rash onset), she developed worsening lower back and abdominal pain (NRS 8) with sleep disturbance. Labs showed CRP 88.8 mg/L, ESR 65 mm/h, WBC 6800/µL with 77.5% neutrophils. Abdominal ultrasound and CT were normal. She was treated for zoster-associated pain. In the fifth week, she was referred to a pain clinic. She reported pain that improved with supine rest but worsened with spinal rotation and postural changes. Physical examination revealed marked midline tenderness over lumbar spinous processes and reproducible pain with percussion. The vesicular rash had resolved. Labs: CRP 36.64 mg/L, ESR 60 mm/h. Non-contrast MRI of the thoracolumbar spine and bone mineral density testing were performed. BMD: T-score -2.9 at L1-L4 (osteoporosis). MRI showed collapse of the L3 vertebral body with an intravertebral fluid cleft, consistent with Kummell's disease. Additional findings: paravertebral soft tissue edema and fluid collection with low-signal rim on T2 and central-to-high-signal on T1 involving the left psoas muscle, suggestive of superimposed infection. Contrast-enhanced MRI demonstrated marked enhancement of paraspinal soft tissues extending into the left psoas muscle. The radiology report indicated differential diagnosis of infectious spondylitis with abscess formation or edema/hematoma related to vertebral compression fracture. Given elevated ESR/CRP and MRI findings, an infectious disease team was consulted. Antibiotics were withheld for 1 week, and CT-guided aspiration biopsy was performed. Bacterial cultures, Gram stain, AFB stain and culture, MTB PCR, NTM PCR, and histopathology were all negative. Based on clinical presentation and imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis?
title: Concomitant osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture and infectious spondylitis in a patient with herpes zoster: A case report

challenge_prompt:
An 18-year-old North African female living in Belgium presents to the emergency room with a four-day history of abdominal pain, cramps, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, asthenia, headaches, slight photophobia, and blurred vision. She reports chills without documented fever. She has no urinary complaints, no recent travel, and no past medical history. During a recent visit to her general practitioner, new-onset hypertension and bilateral lower extremity edema were noted, along with significant weight gain and mild hypercholesterolemia. She is a high school student with a sedentary lifestyle and frequent processed food intake. She does not use alcohol or tobacco.

On examination, blood pressure is 180/128 mmHg, heart rate 127 bpm, afebrile, oxygen saturation normal. She appears pale and tired. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Abdomen slightly tender. Lower limb edema present. Neurological exam is normal, no neck stiffness, no deficits.

Initial laboratory findings: hypochromic normocytic anemia, mild inflammatory syndrome, severe acute renal failure (creatinine 5.96 mg/dL, eGFR 10 mL/min/1.73 m²), hyperphosphatemia, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, hypoalbuminemia (27 g/L), marked hypercholesterolemia. Beta-hCG, HIV serology, and toxicology screen negative. ECG and echocardiography normal.

In the ER, she has a tonic-clonic seizure that resolves spontaneously after one minute, followed by altered consciousness and severe agitation. Cerebral CT with contrast shows no hemorrhage or vascular abnormality. Lumbar puncture: crystal-clear CSF, normal opening pressure, 5 nucleated cells, low glucose (hypoglycorrhachia), normal protein and lactate. CSF multiplex panel negative.

Brain MRI on the same day shows hyperintensities in the occipital lobes and basal nuclei on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences, with a symmetrical aspect, no evidence of venous sinus thrombosis, ischemia, or hemorrhage. EEG shows non-specific encephalopathy without epileptic activity.

Urine analysis: nephrotic-range proteinuria with protein-to-creatinine ratio 12.07 g/g, microscopic hematuria. Renal ultrasound normal.

The following day, autoimmune panel shows speckled antinuclear antibodies (ANA) positive at 1:320, and positive anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA) antibodies.
title: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES) as the Initial Presentation of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus With Lupus Nephritis: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A female infant born at 36 weeks gestation (birth weight 1810 g, small for gestational age) with intrauterine growth restriction and no prenatal care. On day of life 9, she developed respiratory distress requiring non-invasive positive pressure ventilation. Echocardiogram on day 10 showed significantly elevated right ventricular (RV) and pulmonary artery (PA) pressures, a moderate patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), moderate atrial septal defect (ASD), and small mid-muscular ventricular septal defect (VSD). She had multiple congenital anomalies: cleft palate, cerebral artery anomaly, severe right pelvocaliectasis, and hemangiomas. Genetic testing (microarray and whole-exome sequencing) was negative. CT scan showed right heart, main and branch PA dilation, no evidence of interstitial lung disease (ILD), pulmonary veno-occlusive disease (PVOD), or pulmonary capillary hemangiomatosis (PCH). At 5 weeks, cardiac catheterization: Qp:Qs 3:1, RV pressure 60/6 mmHg, mean PA pressure (mPAP) 41 mmHg, pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PAWP) 7 mmHg, descending aorta 60/27 mmHg, indexed pulmonary vascular resistance (PVRi) ~4 WU·m². Transcatheter PDA closure was performed. Despite this, follow-up echocardiograms showed persistent systemic RV/PA pressures, bidirectional shunting across VSD and ASD, mild RV hypertrophy, moderate RV dilation. She was discharged at 9 weeks on 0.25 L/min oxygen. Gradual deterioration: decreased feeding, failure to thrive, increasing oxygen requirement, functional class IIIa–IIIb (PVRI/WHO). At 4 months, repeat catheterization: Qp:Qs 1:1, suprasystemic RV pressure 86/9 mmHg, mPAP 60 mmHg, PAWP 12 mmHg, descending aorta 64/38 mmHg, PVRi ~10 WU·m². Inhaled nitric oxide (iNO) testing minimally improved PVRi to 9 WU·m². Distal pulmonary vasculature was markedly abnormal with tortuous, plump, pulsatile vessels and minimal capillary filling. Repeat CT again negative for ILD, PVOD, PCH. She required prolonged intubation and pulmonary hypertensive crises post-catheterization. Therapy with sildenafil and bosentan was started, and intravenous treprostinil was needed to wean off iNO (uptitrated to 44 ng/kg/min over 10 days). Echocardiograms continued to show systemic to suprasystemic RV/PA pressures, severe RV dilation, moderate RV hypertrophy, mildly diminished RV systolic function. She was extubated and discharged at 5 months on sildenafil 1 mg/kg q6h, bosentan 2 mg/kg q12h, and treprostinil 64 ng/kg/min. Despite further treprostinil uptitration to 90 ng/kg/min, at 7 months she had suprasystemic RV pressure, worsening tricuspid regurgitation (moderate to severe), moderately decreased RV systolic function, clinical deterioration (desaturation, increased work of breathing, failure to thrive), and functional class IIIb. She was deemed ineligible for Potts shunt due to low weight, and family declined lung transplant evaluation. Based on the presentation and course, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
title: Sotatercept administration in a young infant with severe pulmonary arterial hypertension: A case report

challenge_prompt: A previously healthy 24-year-old woman was found unresponsive by her boyfriend after reportedly consuming nearly an entire 15-mL bottle of MIT45 Super K Extra Strong, a highly concentrated kratom extract. She had not slept in several days and developed generalized shaking movements with urinary incontinence before becoming unresponsive. Emergency medical services administered naloxone and midazolam en route. She was intubated in the field due to ongoing seizure-like activity. Shortly after intubation, she became pulseless with a wide-complex rhythm. On arrival to the ED, she remained pulseless with a wide-complex monomorphic rhythm consistent with ventricular tachycardia. High-quality CPR was continued. She underwent multiple rounds of standard defibrillation with brief episodes of return of spontaneous circulation on approximately four occasions, each time degenerating back into pulseless ventricular tachycardia within seconds. At no point did she achieve sustained sinus rhythm. Pharmacologic interventions included a 300-mg amiodarone bolus with continuous infusion, lidocaine infusion at 2 mg/minute, 4 grams of magnesium sulfate, 4 grams of calcium chloride followed by 2 grams of calcium gluconate, 175 mEq of sodium bicarbonate, aggressive potassium repletion, multiple 1-mg epinephrine boluses, and an esmolol bolus. Despite these measures, she remained in refractory ventricular tachycardia. Three dual-sequential defibrillation attempts were performed; none resulted in conversion to a stable perfusing rhythm. Laboratory evaluation revealed: potassium 1.9 mEq/L (3.5–5.1), sodium 150 mEq/L (135–145), arterial pH 7.18 (7.35–7.45), lactate 12.2 mmol/L (0.5–2.0), glucose 240 mg/dL (70–110), aspartate aminotransferase 210 U/L (10–40), alanine aminotransferase 180 U/L (7–56), creatine kinase 912 U/L (30–200). A urine drug screen was positive only for benzodiazepines, consistent with prehospital midazolam administration. Computed tomography of the head showed no acute intracranial process. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated an acute nondisplaced sternal fracture and fat stranding about the pancreas and duodenum concerning for acute interstitial edematous pancreatitis. Electrocardiography on arrival demonstrated wide complex tachycardia consistent with ventricular arrhythmia in the setting of electrolyte derangement.
title: Ventricular Tachycardia Following Kratom Ingestion Requiring Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation in a Young Woman: Case Report

challenge_prompt: An 89-year-old Asian man with a history of left upper lung adenocarcinoma (EGFR Ex19 DEL mutation, negative for ALK and ROS1) on almonertinib 110 mg daily and bevacizumab, and schizophrenia on olanzapine 10 mg daily and zopiclone 7.5 mg daily, presents with a 1-week history of progressively worsening chest tightness and dyspnea. Three months ago, he was hospitalized for acute myocardial infarction, underwent coronary stent placement, and was started on atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. Shortly after starting atorvastatin, he developed elevated creatine kinase (CK) and liver enzymes; both atorvastatin and almonertinib were discontinued, and his enzymes normalized after 16 days. Almonertinib was later restarted at 55 mg then increased to 110 mg daily, with only mild asymptomatic CK elevation. On current admission, vital signs are stable. Physical exam reveals coarse breath sounds with bilateral lower-lobe wet rales, no peripheral edema. Labs: hs-cTnT 0.075 ng/mL (normal <0.014), NT-proBNP 34,209 pg/mL (normal <900), CK 495 U/L (normal <164), normal CK-MB, ALT, AST. ECG: sinus rhythm, first-degree AV block. Echocardiogram: LVEF 30-40%, left atrial enlargement, abnormal wall motion. CT chest shows a parafoveal lesion in the left interlobar fissure. On day 2, atorvastatin 20 mg daily is added for LDL 2.57 mmol/L. On day 3, labs show hs-cTnT 0.124 ng/mL, CK 2103 U/L, CK-MB 43 U/L (normal <24). The patient reports muscle weakness, no myalgia, no recent exercise or trauma. On day 4, serum creatinine rises from 184 to 219.3 μmol/L. What is the most likely diagnosis/etiology, and what is the next appropriate diagnostic or management step?
title: Atorvastatin and almonertinib-induced myopathy in a polypharmacy context: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 68-year-old man with no past medical history presented with progressive generalized weakness, poor oral intake, nausea, vomiting for one week, and a 14 kg weight loss from a baseline of 70 kg. He reported new onset right facial numbness and orbital symptoms including proptosis developing over approximately one week. He noted a progressively enlarging gingival lesion prior to presentation. His last medical evaluation was five years ago and last dental visit three years ago.

Examination: chronically ill appearance; HEENT showed edema and a palpable mass in the right maxillary region with right-sided proptosis. Neurologic exam revealed decreased sensation over superior nasal and supraorbital distribution (CN V1). Ophthalmologic exam: right-eye proptosis, conjunctival injection with inferior chemosis, diffuse extraocular movement restriction, decreased visual acuity (20/200 OD improving to 20/50). Left eye had chronic vision loss from remote trauma. Fundoscopic exam: normal optic disc without edema.

MRI brain with and without contrast: large heterogeneously enhancing necrotic mass centered in the right maxillary sinus measuring 7.1×5.7×5.2 cm, destroying sinus walls and extending into nasal cavity, ethmoid and sphenoid sinuses, right orbit (invading extraocular muscles, orbital apex, optic nerve), cavernous sinus with encasement of internal carotid artery, perineural spread along trigeminal nerve into Meckel's cave and pterygopalatine fossa, involvement of skull base foramina (foramen rotundum, ovale), hard and soft palate, nasopharynx, and masticator space. No acute intracranial hemorrhage or hydrocephalus. Metastasis to right occipital bone and a 2.6 cm level II cervical lymph node.

CT head: large multicomponent multilobulated enhancing destructive/infiltrative mass centered in right maxillary sinus with osseous erosion and vascular encasement. CT abdomen/pelvis: lytic lesions on acetabulum suspicious for metastases.

Biopsy of right upper gingival lesion: invasive moderately differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma with stromal invasion.

EKG: sinus bradycardia with occasional PVCs and low-voltage QRS. Labs: mild anemia and hypoalbuminemia.

Multidisciplinary tumor board (otolaryngology, neurosurgery, medical oncology, radiation oncology, ophthalmology) deemed the tumor unresectable due to infiltration of critical neurovascular structures and intracranial extension.

Based on this information, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next management step?

challenge_prompt: A 68-year-old woman with no systemic illness and no hypertension, diabetes, or atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease presented with unilateral optic disc swelling in her right eye. Six days before presentation, she underwent cervical manipulation at a chiropractic clinic for numbness in her right hand. The following day, she experienced subtle visual distortions and discomfort in her right eye. Two days before referral, a local ophthalmologist noted optic disc edema in the right eye. At presentation, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and 20/13 in the left eye. There was no pain with ocular movements and no relative afferent pupillary defect. Central flicker fusion frequency was normal. Humphrey 24-2 visual field testing showed mild enlargement of the physiological blind spot extending nasosuperiorly in the right eye; the left visual field was normal. Fundus examination and multicolor fundus imaging of the right eye revealed severe inflammation of the optic nerve head with retinal venous vasodilatation and tortuosity, but no hemorrhage in any quadrant. Fluorescein angiography showed late dye leakage from the optic disc with no evidence of venous occlusion, capillary nonperfusion, or ischemic retinal changes. Spectral-domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) confirmed marked papillary edema without macular involvement; average peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer thickness was 156 µm. Intraocular pressure was 13 mmHg in both eyes. Laboratory testing (angiotensin-converting enzyme, anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody, antinuclear antibody, complete blood count, syphilis serology, C-reactive protein) was unremarkable except for an anti-aquaporin-4 antibody level of 3.8 units/L (upper limit of normal 3.0), which was considered clinically insignificant. Five days later, brain and orbital magnetic resonance imaging was normal, with no mass lesions, demyelination, venous sinus thrombosis, or signs of raised intracranial pressure. Differential diagnoses including non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, optic neuritis, papilledema, and incipient central retinal vein occlusion were considered but excluded based on preserved visual acuity, absence of pain with eye movement, unilateral involvement, and normal neuroimaging. Giant cell arteritis was considered unlikely given absence of systemic symptoms and normal inflammatory markers. Visual acuity, color vision, and pupillary reflexes remained stable, but subsequent fundus examination showed progressive optic disc edema with irregular venous caliber and increased vascular tortuosity.
title: Papillophlebitis Presenting As Optic Disc Edema Following Cervical Manipulation: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 66-year-old Chinese woman, a farmer, presents with a 17-day history of diplopia. The diplopia is exacerbated by upward gaze and is accompanied by blurred vision and intermittent throbbing pain. She denies any cardiopulmonary symptoms, including chest pain, palpitations, or syncope. Her medical history is unremarkable except for chronic gastritis; she has no history of hypertension, diabetes, or other systemic illnesses. She has not received any immunomodulatory therapy prior to admission.

On examination, blood pressure is 105/70 mmHg, heart rate 71 beats/min, respiratory rate 11 breaths/min, temperature 36.5°C. Neurological examination shows bilateral ptosis and restricted adduction and abduction of the right eye, with no other focal signs. Best-corrected visual acuity is 0.8 in both eyes. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light (3 mm bilaterally). Extraocular motility: right eye shows restricted adduction, abduction, and upgaze; left eye shows restricted upgaze; downgaze and convergence are normal bilaterally. Diplopia testing reveals vertical diplopia on upgaze and right gaze, and horizontal diplopia on left and right gaze. Anterior segment and funduscopic examinations are unremarkable.

Admission laboratory tests show: plasma fibrinogen 4.70 g/L (reference range 2–4), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 59 mm/h (0–20). Autoantibody profile: homogeneous antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer 1:100 (<1:100); anti-dsDNA antibody 38.10 IU/mL (0–10); all other antibodies are within normal limits. Complement C3 is mildly decreased at 0.79 g/L (0.8–1.5). Cerebrospinal fluid analysis shows mildly elevated protein (0.43 g/L, reference 0–0.4) and IgG (56.40 mg/L, reference 0–34), with other parameters normal. Myocardial injury markers are significantly elevated: creatine kinase (CK) 1507 U/L (40–200), CK-MB 61.92 ng/mL (0–5), high-sensitivity troponin I (hs-cTnI) 1.4868 ng/mL (0–0.0175), B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) 576 pg/mL (0–100), and myoglobin 258.60 ng/mL (1.5–70). A comprehensive panel of infectious, autoimmune, and neoplastic markers – including viral antibodies (Epstein–Barr virus, rubella, respiratory syncytial virus, adenovirus, cytomegalovirus), tumor markers, complement C4, vasculitis panel, quantitative immunoglobulin, and anti-ganglioside antibodies – is unremarkable. Acetylcholine receptor antibodies are negative. Thrombophilia screening (lupus anticoagulant, protein S, protein C, antithrombin III) is unremarkable.

Plain and contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain shows a few ischemic changes in the right frontal subcortical region. Orbital MRI (dedicated extraocular muscle imaging) demonstrates localized T2-weighted imaging (T2WI) hyperintensity in the bilateral medial and lateral rectus muscles. Electromyography reveals involvement of the sensory fibers of the bilateral median nerves.

Admission electrocardiogram shows acute anterior wall myocardial infarction-like changes with premature ventricular contractions. Emergency coronary angiography shows no coronary artery stenosis. The patient develops sustained refractory ventricular tachycardia, terminated by intravenous lidocaine. On hospital day 8, bedside transthoracic echocardiography shows impaired left ventricular diastolic function and left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of approximately 53%. Cardiac MRI demonstrates diffuse elevated T1 mapping signal in the left ventricular myocardium, elevated T2 mapping signal in the basal anterior and anterior septal walls, and subendocardial delayed enhancement.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely etiology of her condition, and what is the next most appropriate diagnostic or therapeutic step?

The patient did not fulfill classification criteria for any defined connective tissue disease.
title: Diplopia as the herald of fulminant myocarditis in undifferentiated connective tissue disease: a case report

Repeat inflammatory markers remained unremarkable. Thyroid function tests were initially deranged but normalized without intervention. Severe vitamin D deficiency was noted. Based only on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

challenge_prompt: A 5-year-old girl with a history of rapid-onset weight gain (50 pounds over 6 months) beginning after a viral-like illness in toddlerhood. At that time, thyroid function tests were normal, and a 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test was appropriately suppressed. Linear growth velocity increased from 35th to 99th percentile by age 2 years. Despite dietary restriction (900 calories/day), hyperphagia continued. She subsequently developed chronic hypernatremia, hypoventilation, and hyperthermia (baseline temperature ~38°C). Developmental milestones were normal. Genetic testing for Prader-Willi syndrome, congenital central hypoventilation syndrome (PHOX2B), and whole exome sequencing were negative. Cerebrospinal fluid autoimmune encephalopathy panel was negative. CT brain without contrast was normal. Neural crest tumor screening (urine VMA, chest X-ray, abdominal ultrasound) was negative. A sleep study showed an apnea/hypopnea index of 7.9 events/hour (sleep apnea) without hypercarbia (transcutaneous CO2 33-39 mmHg). Prolactin was elevated at 71.92 ng/mL (reference 3.34-26.72). No family history of severe obesity. Three years later, she presented with tachypnea (respiratory rate low 40s), fever to 40.8°C, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and malaise. She was found to have bilateral pulmonary emboli and sepsis. Respiratory failure with acidosis (pH 6.99) required intubation. Her weight was >99th percentile throughout. On hospital day 10, psychiatry was consulted for acute change in mental status: inability to sustain eye contact, inconsistent following commands, anxiety, tearfulness, pulling at lines, and staring at things others could not see. At night, she had insomnia, purposeless arm/leg movements, and line pulling. Cornell Assessment of Pediatric Delirium (CAPD) score was 22 (threshold 9). Past psychiatric history: generalized anxiety disorder and recurrent nocturnal episodes beginning in early childhood, ongoing for 1.5 years, typically between 12-4 AM, lasting 15 minutes to 3 hours, with vocalized screaming at home. Nocturnal symptoms were acute in onset during the first third of the sleep period, episodic, with persistent limb thrashing, inconsolability, and similarity to home episodes. No EEG or polysomnography was performed during episodes. She was on BiPAP at home (20/12 mmHg). Medications included fentanyl. What is the most likely cause of the nocturnal behavioral disturbances, and what is the next appropriate management step?
title: Night Terrors and Delirium in a Child With Presumed Rapid-Onset Obesity With Hypothalamic Dysfunction, Hypoventilation, and Autonomic Dysregulation

challenge_prompt: A 4-year-old female Siberian tiger (Panthera tigris altaica) presented with a 4-month history of atypical behavior, disorientation, and suspected visual impairment since arrival at a zoo. Behavioral changes were initially attributed to environmental stress. The animal had not received any medications prior to examination. On remote observation, the tiger did not respond to visual stimuli, relied on auditory and olfactory cues, and exhibited bilateral convergent strabismus. No visual avoidance response was elicited. The tiger was anesthetized for ophthalmological examination. Under anesthesia, both eyeballs were distinctly firm on digital palpation. Pupils were persistently dilated and did not respond to direct or consensual light stimulation. Intraocular pressure measured by rebound tonometry was 48 mmHg in the left eye and 52 mmHg in the right eye. Slit-lamp biomicroscopy revealed a clear anterior chamber, normal iris, and an open iridocorneal angle by direct observation. Mild lens opacities were present. Funduscopy showed bilateral extensive retinopathy with a map-like pattern and a focal degenerative lesion in the left eye. The optic nerve head appeared unremarkable. B-mode ocular ultrasonography demonstrated a normal posterior pole contour with no evidence of retinal detachment, intraocular hemorrhage, or mass. During anesthetic recovery, the tiger experienced several generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Keepers also reported recurrent brief episodes of tremor, head-striking, and motor incoordination since the animal's arrival. challenge_prompt:
A 44-year-old woman presented with insidious onset of gait imbalance over one month, neck pain radiating to the right upper limb for four weeks, and new-onset bladder hesitancy for one week. She had low-grade fever for two days. Neurological examination revealed brisk upper and lower limb reflexes, hypertonia, normal power, positive Hoffmann sign and inverted supinator jerk bilaterally, positive tandem gait and Romberg tests, and a Nurick grade 3 with mJOA score of 16. Sensation and bowel/bladder control were intact. Blood work showed WBC 10,300/mm3 and CRP 185 mg/L. Preoperative cervical radiographs were normal. MRI revealed vertebral body edema, signal changes at C4-5 disc, an anterior epidural collection extending behind C5, cord compression from C4 to C6, and cord edema, reported as C4-5 spondylodiscitis with anterior epidural abscess. The patient underwent C5 corpectomy with C4-6 fusion. Intraoperatively, a frank purulent collection was encountered; no dural defect or CSF leak was noted. On postoperative day 2, the patient developed mild right-hand grip weakness (MRC 4/5 in C7-T1 distribution) with mild numbness in that distribution. Postoperative sagittal T2w MRI showed ventral displacement of the cord at C5-6 with focal anterior angulation, tethering of the cord to the anterior thecal sac, and a widened dorsal CSF column. No CSF leak was evident clinically or from drain output. Histopathology of the intraoperative specimen showed acid-fast bacilli, and GeneXpert detected rifampicin-sensitive Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis for the postoperative MRI finding, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
title: Ventral Spinal Cord Displacement After Single-Level Anterior Cervical Corpectomy: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 65-year-old woman with newly diagnosed common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) and a trough immunoglobulin G level of 235 mg/dL was started on a 35-g weight-based dose of intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) (immune globulin infusion, human, 10%; Gammagard Liquid). Within hours of receiving half the dose, she presented with nerve pain, severe itching, a rash, tachycardia, and hypertension, and was diagnosed with red man syndrome. She was then switched to subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) (Cuvitru) 10 g once weekly. Within 2 hours after the first SCIG dose, she experienced severe neuropathy described as a burning/pins-and-needles sensation in the extremities and muscle twitching, which lasted for several days. She did not report edema, headache, or fatigue. Due to the low IgG levels and inability to tolerate IRT, her health care provider requested insurance authorization for a trial of off-label therapy, but the request was denied. Subsequently, the patient was admitted to the intensive care unit for bilateral pneumonia and sepsis. After recovery, the provider wishes to restart SCIG therapy. challenge_prompt:
A 52-year-old man presents with progressive polymyalgia, leukopenia, and yellow-brown cutaneous plaques. He has a history of coronary artery disease with stenting in 2011 and mild leukopenia since 2017. In 2019, a skin biopsy of axillary plaques showed xanthomatosis with dermal foamy histiocytes. Cardiac MRI in 2021 revealed asymmetric septal hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction (66%). Over the next two years, leukopenia worsened, diffuse myalgia developed, and he lost approximately 10 kg. On admission in late 2023, blood pressure was asymmetric (right 139/85 mmHg, left 115/70 mmHg). Physical exam shows multiple yellow-brown plaques and hyperpigmented patches over periorbital areas, neck, shoulders, axillae, and groins, a grade 3/6 systolic murmur at mitral and aortic areas, and a diminished left radial pulse. Laboratory results: white blood cell count 1.67 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 104 g/L, platelets 128 × 10^9/L. Serum electrophoresis reveals an M-spike (IgG 12.4 g/L) and type I cryoglobulinemia (3.5%, 3.9 g/L) of IgG-λ component. Free κ/λ ratio 0.178 (κ 23.0 mg/L, λ 129.6 mg/L). Urine immunofixation detects λ light chains. Hepatitis C and autoimmune workup negative. Creatine kinase normal; post-exercise lactate 3.7 mmol/L. Positive Coombs test; low complement C3 0.7 g/L, C4 0.021 g/L; ANA 1:80, anti-dsDNA 516 IU/mL; ESR 77 mm/h, CRP 16.8 mg/L, IL-6 17.6 pg/mL. Bone marrow aspiration shows 1.4% λ-restricted clonal plasma cells with normal cytogenetics. PET-CT shows mild splenomegaly and diffuse marrow uptake without lytic lesions. Skin biopsy from posterior neck: mild atrophy, basal layer hyperpigmentation, and clusters of foamy histiocytes in superficial dermis; negative Congo red. Muscle MRI: patchy T2 hyperintensity in quadriceps and gluteal muscles. Electromyography normal. Muscle biopsy: myofibrillar disarray with scattered inflammatory infiltrates, negative Congo red, negative BRAF V600E mutation, ATPase staining shows predominance of type II muscle fibers. Whole-exome sequencing no relevant pathogenic mutations. Echocardiography: concentric hypertrophy with preserved systolic function and mild valvular regurgitation. Cardiac MRI: asymmetric septal thickening with late gadolinium enhancement. Multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, lymphoma, and myelodysplasia have been excluded.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 50-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with a four-day history of fever and productive cough with purulent sputum. On admission, she was febrile, tachycardic, and in severe respiratory distress, requiring immediate transfer to the intensive care unit. Chest CT revealed cylindrical bronchiectasis with a characteristic tram-track appearance. Initial laboratory investigations demonstrated marked leukocytosis (19,960/mm³, neutrophils 90%) and elevated C-reactive protein (160 mg/dL). Renal function and electrolytes were within normal limits. Respiratory microbiological samples identified Streptococcus pneumoniae and Influenza A virus. Blood cultures remained negative. The patient required intubation and mechanical ventilation and subsequently developed ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ <200 mmHg). Empirical antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone and amikacin was initiated. During the ICU course, she developed septic shock requiring norepinephrine infusion. On hospital day 5, neurological deterioration occurred, characterized by generalized seizures, nystagmus, and altered mental status. Brain MRI demonstrated multiple multiloculated cerebral abscesses involving cortico-subcortical regions, with ring enhancement, diffusion restriction, vasogenic edema, and mild mass effect, consistent with pyogenic abscesses. HIV serology was negative. Transthoracic echocardiography showed no evidence of infective endocarditis; transesophageal echocardiography was not performed. Chest X-ray showed known bronchiectasis without new abnormalities.

Based on the above presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
title: Multiple Brain Abscesses Associated With Severe Bronchiectasis and Respiratory Infection: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 12-year-old Caucasian female is referred for evaluation of suspected sectoral edema in her right eye. Previous perimetry, intravenous fluorescein angiography, neuroimaging, and Pentacam examinations are normal. She has a prior diagnosis of postural proprioceptive disorder and uses astigmatic correction (−1.00 180°) in both eyes, with prism correction (temporal base 4 PD and upper base 2 PD in the right eye; temporal base 3 PD and upper base 2 PD in the left eye). Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes. Ocular motility shows hyperfunction of the inferior oblique muscle (2/4) bilaterally, with near point of convergence at 15 cm. Biomicroscopy is normal, and Ishihara color plates are normal. Fundoscopy reveals anomalous mild elevation of both optic discs without folds or subretinal fluid; macular and retinal vessels appear normal. OCT B-scan and en face OCT demonstrate some fine tiny drusen and a well-defined intradiscal hyperreflective body without posterior shadow, with no evidence of fluid. OCT-A shows no abnormal vascular abnormalities in vessel patterns, but intralesion vascularity is identified during angio B-mode analysis.
title: Peripapillary Hyperreflective Ovoid Mass‐Like Structure as an Optic Disc Pseudo‐Edema Case Report and Review

challenge_prompt: An 84-year-old man presented with left hemiparesis and impaired consciousness. MRI revealed an acute infarction in the right parietal lobe; dual antiplatelet therapy was initiated. On hospital day six, he developed asymptomatic diffuse ST-segment elevation on ECG. Transthoracic echocardiography showed akinesis of the mid-to-apical segments of the left ventricle with compensatory hyperkinesis of the basal segments (apical ballooning pattern). Coronary angiography revealed approximately 75% stenosis in the proximal right coronary artery (segment #1) and approximately 75% stenosis in the left anterior descending artery (segment #6), not considered flow-limiting. Left ventriculography confirmed wall motion abnormalities extending beyond a single coronary artery territory. Laboratory findings on day six: troponin I 3.79 ng/mL, CK-MB 29.4 ng/mL, BNP 1016 pg/mL, CRP 1.55 mg/dL, WBC 12,600/µL. Several hours later, the patient suddenly developed bradycardia followed by pulseless electrical activity. Echocardiography performed during resuscitation revealed pericardial effusion with fibrin-like echoes adjacent to the left ventricular free wall. What is the most likely diagnosis or etiology of this acute deterioration?
title: Echocardiographic Diagnosis of Ventricular Free Wall Rupture Complicating Takotsubo Syndrome: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 37-year-old male patient was referred to Shahid Beheshti Hospital in Qom Province for hip joint replacement. The patient's vital signs at admission were as follows: BP: 110/70 mmHg, HR: 60/min, RR: 14/min; SpO2 (Room Air): 96%, and the patient's history of drug use and allergy was negative. The patient underwent the procedure under spinal anesthesia. The patient was positioned in the left lateral decubitus position. Under aseptic conditions, loss-of-resistance (LOR) to air was used to identify the epidural space at the L3-L4 interspace with an 18-gauge Tuohy needle. During this procedure, an inadvertent dural puncture was noted with return of clear cerebrospinal fluid. The Tuohy needle was withdrawn. Subsequently, a spinal anesthetic was successfully performed at the L4-L5 interspace using a 25-gauge Quincke needle with the injection of 12.5 mg of 0.5% hyperbaric bupivacaine. The surgery then proceeded uneventfully. The history of the patient's surgery was as follows: the duration of the surgery was 1.5 h, and during the surgery, he experienced 400 cc of bleeding and 400 cc of urine output, and he received 2.5 L of normal saline and Ringer's serum. After recovering from stable vital signs, the patient recovered when his clinical conditions were controlled. During hospitalization within 24 h after surgery, the patient presented with a history of continuous headaches and nausea and vomiting; thus, both anesthesia and neurology consultations were requested.

Based on the above presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what next diagnostic step is most appropriate?
title: Incidence and Management of Pneumocephalus, an Uncommon Complication of Spinal Anesthesia: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 69-year-old female presents with an 8-year history of trunk and limb pain. She was diagnosed with spinal cord cysticercosis 8 years ago and underwent laminectomy. The pain persisted and worsened, described as pulling, burning, and electric shock-like, distributed in patches across trunk and limbs. It is accompanied by sensory disturbances, progressive lower limb weakness predominantly on the right, distal numbness and coldness. Clinical examination reveals hyperalgesia in right thigh, left lower limb, and trunk; sensory deficits in right calf and foot; quadriceps muscle strength graded III on right and IV on left. VAS score 6-7. Spinal MRI shows post-operative changes: spinous processes and laminae absent at T10, T11, T12, L1, L2, with heterogeneous signals and strip-like CSF signals in posterior cauda equina. No vertebral body destruction, disc herniation, or intramedullary abnormalities. She has failed physical therapy, interventional pain management, and multiple analgesic regimens.
title: Case Report: Spinal cord stimulation for neuropathic pain from spinal cysticercosis

challenge_prompt: A 65-year-old man is transported to the emergency department after a motor vehicle collision. On arrival, he is in hemorrhagic shock. Emergent CT demonstrates pulmonary contusions, hemothorax, multiple rib fractures, and fractures of the right femur, left humerus, and L5 vertebral body. He is managed conservatively with fluid resuscitation and blood transfusions. Three days after injury, he develops sudden shock with severe chest pain. Contrast-enhanced CT shows a right hemothorax and rib fracture without contrast extravasation, but there is rapid increase in hematoma drainage from the chest tube. Because surgical hemostasis is expected to be delayed, transcatheter arterial embolization (TAE) is attempted. Selective angiography of the right 10th intercostal artery reveals a small pseudoaneurysm in the distal segment at the level of the rib fracture. The Adamkiewicz artery (AKA) is identified, originating from the proximal portion of the right 10th intercostal artery. What is the most appropriate next step in performing TAE to minimize the risk of spinal cord ischemia?
title: Intercostal artery embolization and the risk of spinal cord ischemia: Importance of identifying the Adamkiewicz artery—A report of two cases

challenge_prompt:
A 36-year-old African man with no known comorbidities presented to the emergency department with a two-week history of polyuria, polydipsia, and progressive dyspnea. He denied fever, cough, vomiting, diarrhea, chest pain, or headache. He had no history of toxin ingestion or traditional medicine use. Social history: recreational alcohol use, no recent consumption.

On examination, he was awake and alert, with Kussmaul breathing, oxygen saturation normal on room air, heart rate 112, blood pressure 140/78, no signs of shock. Neurologically, he was fully oriented with equal reactive pupils and no focal deficits. No meningism. Initial chest X-ray was normal. Venous blood gas showed severe high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (anion gap 29) with partially compensated respiratory alkalosis. Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) revealed normal heart size, underfilled left ventricle, hyperdynamic function, IVC collapse >50% (volume depletion), bilateral A-lines, no B-lines or effusions, normal renal morphology, compressible femoral and popliteal veins. Initial labs confirmed hyperglycemia, ketonemia, and metabolic acidosis consistent with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). CT brain was not performed initially.

He was diagnosed with severe DKA and managed with intravenous fluids (Plasma-Lyte), insulin infusion (0.1 IU/kg/hr), potassium replacement, thiamine, empiric antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanic acid), and enoxaparin. Fluid resuscitation was guided by POCUS. At 11 hours, cumulative fluid intake was 4 liters, but POCUS still indicated intravascular depletion.

He then developed increased work of breathing. Auscultation revealed bilateral coarse crepitations. POCUS showed new bilateral confluent B-lines with preserved lung sliding. Repeat chest X-ray demonstrated bilateral infiltrates, more prominent on the left. Despite these findings, he remained normoxic (SpO2 96% on room air) and normocapnic on arterial blood gas. Maintenance fluid rate was reduced from 1.5 to 1 mL/kg/hr.

Concurrently, his neurological status declined: Glasgow Coma Scale dropped to 12/15 (E4V3M5). CT scan of the brain demonstrated diffuse cerebral swelling with effacement of cerebral sulci and compression of the basal cisterns, without herniation. Osmotherapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline) was deferred, and diuretics were withheld due to ongoing concern for intravascular volume depletion.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next best step in management?
title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis Complicated by Cerebral and Pulmonary Oedema in an Adult

challenge_prompt: A 70-year-old man presents with exertional chest pain and shortness of breath. His cardiovascular risk factors include advanced age and male sex; there is no prior history of stroke or transient ischemic attack. Electrocardiography shows ischemic changes, while high-sensitivity troponin levels are within normal limits. Coronary angiography reveals an 85% proximal stenosis of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery. As part of the preoperative evaluation, carotid duplex ultrasound and computed tomography angiography (CTA) demonstrate critical (approximately 90%) stenosis of the right internal carotid artery (ICA) accompanied by a metallic artifact near the carotid bifurcation. Upon further questioning, the patient recalls sustaining a gunshot wound to the neck 30 years earlier. Imaging localizes the metallic object just below the ICA–external carotid artery (ECA) bifurcation and superficial to the vagus nerve. The patient has no neurological symptoms. Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or treatment step?
title: Patient with a retained bullet near the carotid bifurcation: simultaneous carotid endarterectomy and beating-heart LIMA–LAD bypass in a 70-year-old man
